text: Robotic ports are positioned using a forearm technique, with an assistant port on the right side. The surgeon examines the pelvis and uses a trans-abdominal proline suture to suspend the uterus, facilitating the opening of the recto-vaginal septum. An incision is made on the peritoneum at the sacral promontory.

The surgeon performs a careful dissection to open the lateral total mesorectal excision (TME) plane, avoiding injury to the mesorectal vessels. The hypogastric nerves are identified and preserved. A three-dimensional robotic camera provides enhanced visualization. Monopolar and bipolar diathermy are used to maintain a clear view without bleeding. An incision is made over the peritoneum lining the pouch of Douglas, and the recto-vaginal septum is opened, developing the plane between the rectum and vagina with traction and counter-traction.

The surgeon uses Endo-wrist instruments to access the recto-vaginal septum, carefully dissecting through varying degrees of scarring. The dissection progresses towards the perineal body, where the white fibers of the perineal body become visible, marking the point where the rectum ceases to separate from the vaginal wall.

The surgeon performs a rectal examination to confirm the dissection level at the top of the anal sphincter complex. A 5x15 cm strip of iPalm polyproline mesh with a bowel-friendly coating is prepared for suturing. Using Ethibon II-O suture, the mesh is secured to the rectum with robotic endo-wrist instruments, ensuring adequate bite placement on the rectal wall without full thickness penetration, achieving secure mesh fixation.

The surgeon sutures the mesh to the top of the sacrum using a sliding knot technique to adjust tension, avoiding tackers. Additional sutures are placed to securely anchor the mesh. The peritoneal defect is closed with a running suture of 2-0 V-Loc.
The surgeon ensures that the entire mesh is positioned extraperitoneally at the conclusion of the procedure, confirming its placement outside the peritoneal cavity.
